title: Ground state of the Hubbard model with spin-dependent linear potential
abstract: We investigate the competition between attractive spin-spin interactions and spin-separating external forces in the ground state of a one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model. We consider a lattice with open boundary conditions, subject to a linear external potential whose gradient is opposite for the two spin components, so that each spin species sees a potential minimum at a different end of the lattice. Using density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) simulations, we map the ground-state density distributions and the number of doubly occupied sites as a function of the potential gradient $β$ and interaction strength. We identify three distinct regimes separated by critical threshold gradients: (i) a small-$β$ regime where fermion pairing remains robust against the external potential; (ii) an intermediate-$β$ phase-separated regime characterized by a staircase-like decrease in the doublon number, corresponding to the successive, one-by-one breaking of bound pairs; and (iii) a large-$β$ regime where the two spin components are completely spatially separated. We complement the numerical results with a phenomenological model and a local-density approximation analysis, from which we derive closed-form analytical estimates for these critical threshold values. We also verify that the staircase structure persists under additional harmonic confinement. Our results are directly testable in cold-atom experiments, and demonstrate that a spin-dependent linear potential enables precise, integer-level control of the number of bound fermion pairs.
summary: This paper explores how a spin-dependent linear potential affects the stability of fermion pairs in a 1D attractive Hubbard model. It reveals that increasing the potential gradient leads to a discrete, 'staircase' breaking of pairs, transitioning from a paired state to a fully spin-separated state. The authors provide analytical tools to predict these transitions and show that the effect is robust even under harmonic confinement. These findings offer a way to control the number of bound pairs in experimental cold-atom setups.
main_problem: Investigating the competition between attractive spin-spin interactions and spin-dependent external forces in a 1D Fermi-Hubbard model, specifically how a linear potential gradient induces the successive breaking of fermion pairs.
main_result: The study identifies three regimes: robust pairing, a phase-separated regime with a staircase-like decrease in doublon number due to one-by-one pair breaking, and complete spin separation. The authors also provide analytical estimates for the critical threshold gradients.
method: The researchers use Density-Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) simulations, supplemented by a phenomenological model and Local-Density Approximation (LDA) analysis to derive closed-form analytical estimates.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model with attractive on-site interactions (U < 0) and open boundary conditions, subject to a spin-dependent linear potential gradient (beta) that pushes different spin species toward opposite ends of the lattice.
key_observables: Doublon number (number of doubly occupied sites), spin-resolved density distributions, local relative spin polarization, and critical threshold gradients (beta_c1 and beta_c2).
important_parameters: Potential gradient strength (beta), interaction strength (U), lattice size (L), filling fraction/density (n), and harmonic confinement strength (w).
main_assumptions: The system is focused on balanced spin populations (N_up = N_down); the staircase structure is intrinsically tied to finite system size and open boundaries; the LDA is unable to capture the discrete stepwise descent in the number of pairs.
figures_summary: Figures show the dependence of the doublon number on beta for various U values, the evolution of critical thresholds (beta_c1, beta_c2) as functions of U and density, and a comparison between DMRG and LDA predictions for density profiles and phase boundaries.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the many-body Hamiltonian and symmetry properties, moves to single-particle spectrum analysis and exact diagonalization, presents large-scale DMRG results for density and pair-breaking, and concludes with analytical modeling and LDA-based threshold estimations.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it demonstrates a method for precise, integer-level control of bound fermion pairs using spin-dependent potentials, a technique directly testable in cold-atom experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Top row: The many-body eigenenergy spectrum as a function of β for (a) U = −15, (b) U = −2.5, (c) U = 0, for a small system with size L = 8 and population N↑+ N↓= 2 + 2. The colors indicate the doublon number ND in each eigenstate (as per the color scale at the top). In (a), the ground-state anticrossings with nearby eigenstates are shown closely in the insets. For clarity, the ground state is plotted with a thicker line in (b), (c), and the insets of (a). Bottom row: The doublon number ND in the ground eigenstate as a function of β, for the values of U corresponding to the upper panels.

FIG. 2. (a) The ground-state doublon number ND, as a func- tion of U and β, for L = 120 and N↑+ N↓= 10 + 10. (b) The corresponding rescaled doublon number N ′ D [Eq. (12)], which approximates the total number of bound pairs. (c,d) ND and N ′ D for L = 120 and N = 45 + 45.

FIG. 3. (a) Ground-state rescaled doublon number N ′ D as function of β, for an example system with L = 120, U = −2, N = 10 + 10. (b) Ground-state density profiles ⟨ˆnj,↑⟩(upper) and ⟨ˆnj,↓⟩(lower) at sites j = 1, . . . , L as a function of β, for the same system. The local density on each site is indicated by a color scale. The two dotted lines mark the critical gra- dient values, βc1 and βc2, at which the ground-state density undergoes a sudden change in behavior. (c, d, e) Example density profiles for three different values of β, representing: (c) the small-gradient regime β < βc1, (d) the intermediate- gradient regime βc1 ≤β < βc2, and (e) the large-gradient regime βc2 ≤β. The value of...
FIG. 4. Same as Fig. 3, but for U = −0.5.

FIG. 5. (a,b) Threshold values βc1 and βc2 as a function of U, for L = 120 and (a) N = 10 + 10 or (b) N = 45 + 45. (c,d) Same, as a function of n, for a system with L = 120 and (c) U = −1 or (d) U = −4; note the logarithmic scale. (e,f) Same, as a function of 1/L, for a system with U = −4 and (e) n = L/12 + L/12 or (f) N = 3L/8 + 3L/8. The dashed lines indicate linear fits ∝1/L, fitted in the range 1/L ≤0.01; these fits show that βc1, βc2 reach 0 in the 1/L →0 limit.

FIG. 6. (a) The estimated energy EM(β) (from Eq. (13)) for all possible pair numbers M = 0, . . . , 10, for a system with L = 120, U = −2, N = 10 + 10. Red arrows, and their accompanying labels, indicate the values of β at which adja- cent lowest-energy curves cross. (b) Comparison between the DMRG ground-state energy and the phenomenological model estimate, obtained as the minimum of EM(β) over M. Inset: The difference between the two energy predictions.

FIG. 7. Rescaled doublon number N ′ D from DMRG (green line), and predicted pair number M from the phenomenologi- cal model, given by M : min [EM(β)] (diamond symbols), for three values of U with L = 120, N = 10 + 10.

FIG. 8. (a) Threshold value βc1 as a function of |U| for L = 120, N = 45 + 45, obtained in three ways: from DMRG (continuous line), from the analytical estimate in Eq. (16) (dashed line), and from substituting numerically determined En(β) into Eq. (14) (diamonds). (b) Threshold values βc2 obtained from DMRG (continuous line), from the analytical estimate in Eq. (19) (dashed line), and from substituting nu- merically determined En(β) into Eq. (15) (diamonds). (c,d) Same for N = 10 + 10.

FIG. 9. Example of predicting the density profile via the local-density approximation. (a) Phase diagram of the grand- canonical Hubbard model, in the µ-h plane, for U = −5 (see Appendix C for explanation of phase labels). The two trajec- tories across the phase diagram correspond to lattice systems with the parameters U = −5, N = 45 + 45, L = 120, and two different gradients: β = 0.1, β = 0.234. The two trajec- tories are defined by the following coordinates: µ = −2.867, h(1) = 5.95, h(L) = −5.95, and: µ = −3.535, h(1) = 13.923, h(L) = −13.923, respectively.
